answer:
## Tích hợp Claude Code + Warp + HERDR cho remote-coding tool (libghostty/NetBird)

Toàn bộ phân tích dưới đây dựa trên corpus đã được adversarial-verified. Tôi đánh dấu rõ những claim đã bị **refuted** hoặc còn **uncertain** ở từng chỗ load-bearing.

---

### 1. Tích hợp Claude Code: TUI requirements + có nên đi sâu qua Agent SDK?

#### 1.1. Claude Code là gì (về mặt terminal)
Claude Code là một **native binary** (x64/ARM64, cài vào `~/.local/bin/claude`), KHÔNG phải Node.js TUI wrapper — npm chỉ là kênh phân phối ([code.claude.com/docs/en/setup](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/setup)). Nó cần một **real PTY** trên Unix (đọc terminal dimensions, emit escape sequences, dùng alt-screen). Đây là tin tốt cho kiến trúc của bạn: raw byte stream qua NetBird hoạt động *nếu* transport layer trung thành với PTY signals (SIGWINCH/`TIOCSWINSZ`) và không strip escape sequences.

Có **hai render mode**:
- **Inline-scrollback (default)**: append vào scrollback của host terminal. Mode này có bug SIGWINCH nổi tiếng — mỗi resize ghi một frame mới mà không xóa frame cũ, flood scrollback ([issue #49086](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/49086), [#20094](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/20094)).
- **Fullscreen alt-screen (opt-in)**: `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` hoặc `/tui fullscreen` (cần v2.1.89+) — dùng alternate screen buffer như vim, memory phẳng, ít byte/frame, thêm mouse support ([code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen)).

> **Với remote PTY ở bất kỳ latency non-trivial nào, fullscreen mode là mode đúng duy nhất.** Nó isolate redraw vào alt-screen và giảm byte/frame — trực tiếp giúp qua WireGuard. Bật mặc định.

#### 1.2. TUI requirements — MUST support (theo thứ tự ưu tiên)

| # | Yêu cầu | Lý do / nguồn |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Propagate `COLORTERM=truecolor` vào PTY env** | UI elements (spinner, permission borders, diff bg, statusline) là hardcoded 24-bit ANSI. Remote shell thường không advertise COLORTERM → màu washed-out ([terminal-config](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/terminal-config), [#35806](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/35806)) |
| 2 | **Set `TERM=xterm-ghostty`** | Đây là TERM native của libghostty; bật kitty keyboard protocol cho client. ⚠️ Xem caveat DEC 2026 ở dưới |
| 3 | **Forward kitty keyboard protocol reports nguyên vẹn** | Shift+Enter, Option+Enter, modifier combos phụ thuộc vào nó. Ctrl+J luôn chèn newline mọi terminal ([interactive-mode](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/interactive-mode)) |
| 4 | **Bật fullscreen mode mặc định** (`CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`) | Như trên |
| 5 | **Forward SGR mouse tracking reports** | Fullscreen mode request mouse; click-to-position, click-expand tool results, drag-select → OSC 52 copy |
| 6 | **Pass OSC 52 + OSC 8 nguyên vẹn** qua TCP byte stream | Clipboard copy + clickable hyperlinks. ĐỪNG rewrite/strip ([#21586](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21586), [fullscreen](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen)) |
| 7 | **Forward SIGWINCH + update PTY ioctl, debounce ~50ms** | Giảm flood redraw qua WireGuard |
| 8 | **Forward Ctrl+C / Ctrl+D / Esc / double-Esc KHÔNG translate** | Chúng có hành vi Claude Code-specific (Esc = stop turn, double-Esc = rewind menu), không phải POSIX default ([interactive-mode](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/interactive-mode)) |
| 9 | **Bracketed paste** (mode 2004) wrappers `ESC[200~`/`ESC[201~` | Paste >10.000 ký tự collapse thành `[Pasted text]` placeholder; `-p` mode cap stdin 10MB ([headless](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless)) |

#### 1.3. Hai caveat load-bearing (đã verify — đọc kỹ trước khi ship)

- **⚠️ DEC 2026 synchronized output với `xterm-ghostty` — CONFIRMED là vấn đề.** Từ v2.1.110 Claude Code chuyển từ dynamic capability detection sang **hardcoded TERM allowlist**: chỉ gửi DEC 2026 khi TERM *đúng* là `xterm-ghostty` hoặc `xterm-kitty` ([#49584](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/49584), [#55613](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/55613)). Workaround `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1` shipped ở v2.1.129 nhưng **root cause (allowlist thay vì DECRQM) vẫn chưa fix** tới v2.1.159. → **Nếu client của bạn emit một TERM value mới/custom, BẮT BUỘC set `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1`.** Nếu giữ đúng `xterm-ghostty` thì OK natively, nhưng phải canh bug paste tokenization của terminfo `xterm-ghostty` ([#54700](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/54700)) — nếu nó manifest, expose toggle về `xterm-256color` (sẽ tắt DEC 2026 nhưng tránh bug paste).

- **❌ Image paste qua OSC 5522 — REFUTED, KHÔNG ship.** Corpus ban đầu ghi "Ghostty PR in progress"; verdict adversarial cho thấy điều này SAI cả hai chiều: Ghostty PR #10560 đã **MERGED 16/02/2026 (ship trong 1.3.0)** nhưng **chỉ parse-only, KHÔNG implement hành vi** ([PR #10560](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/10560), [1.3.0 release notes](https://ghostty.org/docs/install/release-notes/1-3-0)). Implementation thực sự được accept là [issue #10549](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/issues/10549) với comment "we can figure out how to impl this later" — **chưa có PR**. Claude Code issue #42712 KHÔNG verify được (GitHub trả ISSUE NOT FOUND). → **Đừng ship feature image-paste dựa vào OSC 5522 semantics của Ghostty: Ghostty sẽ silently parse nhưng bỏ qua.** Document đây là known limitation; workaround tạm: macOS native Ctrl+V (không Cmd+V), hoặc Kitty (full support).

#### 1.4. Có nên đi sâu qua Agent SDK? — **CÓ, cho một dedicated pane riêng biệt.** (just-run-TUI vs SDK-driven)

Ba tier integration:
- **Tier TUI (PTY passthrough)** — đầy đủ trải nghiệm interactive, nhưng pixel-push ANSI qua network nhạy latency. Issue [#20286](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/20286) chứng minh ở ~500ms RTT permission dialog đến trễ 30+ phút (đó là VS Code serialization cụ thể; raw PTY của bạn không có bug đó, nhưng React renderer vẫn re-render mỗi token → nhiều small write → latency typing).
- **Tier headless (`claude -p`)** — non-interactive, `--output-format stream-json` emit NDJSON events (text_delta, tool use, system/init), `--json-schema`, `--continue`/`--resume SESSION_ID`, `--bare` (skip `~/.claude` cho reproducibility) ([headless](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless)).
- **Tier Agent SDK** (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` / `claude-agent-sdk`) — `query()` async generator yield typed messages; PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks in-process; subagent definitions; MCP attachment; session resumption. TS SDK **bundle native binary làm optional dep** — không cần cài Claude Code riêng ([agent-sdk/overview](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview)).

> **Khuyến nghị kiến trúc lai:** giữ raw PTY path cho user muốn full TUI; **thêm SDK-driven agent pane** (SwiftUI native) cho structured interaction. SDK output là **JSON over stdout — tolerant với network buffering hơn nhiều so với raw ANSI**. Map: tool invocations → UI cards, text_delta → streaming pane, permission approval → native buttons, session cost/model → status bar. Dùng `--bare` để pane này reproducible bất kể `~/.claude` của user.

> **⚠️ Billing — CONFIRMED:** từ **15/06/2026**, `claude -p` và Agent SDK trên **subscription plan** rút từ "monthly Agent SDK credit" riêng ($20 Pro / $100 Max-5x / $200 Max-20x). **API-key auth (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`) KHÔNG bị ảnh hưởng** — pay-as-you-go như cũ ([support article 15036540](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15036540)). → Nếu tool dùng API-key auth thì không sao; nếu OAuth/subscription, cảnh báo user.

---

### 2. Warp terminal: học được gì — must-have vs nice-to-have vs đừng copy

#### 2.1. Insight cốt lõi
Sức mạnh block model của Warp **đến hoàn toàn từ shell-integration signals, KHÔNG từ renderer**. Warp không phải PTY passthrough emulator: nó own input editor (buffer keystroke ở client, chỉ ghi PTY khi Enter), và group output thành typed blocks qua **injected shell hooks** (precmd/preexec) emit JSON metadata trong escape sequence ([how-warp-works](https://www.warp.dev/blog/how-warp-works), [block-model blog](https://www.warp.dev/blog/block-model-behind-warps-agentic-development-environment)).

> **⚠️ Đính chính protocol (CONFIRMED):** Warp dùng **DCS** (Device Control String, `\eP$f{JSON}\x9c`) làm primary protocol trên **macOS/Linux**, KHÔNG phải OSC 133. Trên **Windows** chuyển sang **custom OSC** vì ConPTY nuốt DCS ([building-warp-on-windows](https://www.warp.dev/blog/building-warp-on-windows)). Quan trọng: **Warp KHÔNG consume OSC 133** — inject OSC 133 markers còn *làm vỡ* render của Warp ([Warp #6718](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp/issues/6718)). Warp đã open-source (28/04/2026, client AGPL v3 + UI crates MIT) nhưng **source Rust terminal emulation chưa có trong public tree** — protocol chỉ verify được từ docs/blog, không phải source.

Điểm mấu chốt cho bạn: bạn **không** dùng protocol DCS proprietary của Warp. Bạn dùng **OSC 133** (chuẩn mở: A=prompt start, B=command start, C=output begins, D=command finished + exit code) mà iTerm2/Ghostty/Kitty/WezTerm/Windows Terminal đều implement. **Ghostty (libghostty) đã implement OSC 133 sẵn** cho bash/zsh/fish/elvish/nushell. Có open issue xin Claude Code emit OSC 133 ([#22528](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/22528)) — **nhưng Claude Code hiện CHƯA emit OSC 133** (claims_to_verify ghi nhận điều này; nhiều issue mở: #1465, #32635). Nghĩa là block detection sẽ áp dụng cho **shell commands** quanh/ngoài Claude Code, không phải nội bộ session Claude Code.

#### 2.2. Phân loại transferable

**MUST-HAVE:**
1. **OSC 133 shell-integration inject trên remote host** — vì PTY chạy qua NetBird TCP (P2P trực tiếp), escape sequences flow nguyên vẹn, **không cần side channel hay remote-server binary** (khác hẳn Warp SSH). Đây là enabling primitive cho block grouping, exit-code coloring, prompt-jump.
2. **Claude Code lifecycle hooks kiểu `claude-code-warp`** — pattern: shell script hook 6 event (SessionStart, Stop, Notification, PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse), emit OSC 777 → drive status pane "agent running / needs permission / done". ⚠️ **CONFIRMED nhưng partially outdated:** format `\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON>\007` đúng cho CC **< 2.1.141**; từ **CC ≥ 2.1.141** plugin chuyển sang field `terminalSequence` JSON trên stdout (vì Stop hook validator reject unknown field), bypass `/dev/tty`. **Implementation phải handle CẢ HAI path** ([warpdotdev/claude-code-warp](https://github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp), `emit-terminal-sequence.sh`).
3. **Bidirectional PTY fidelity gồm control sequences** (Ctrl+C/Z/D, arbitrary byte writes). Warp #1 Terminal-Bench (52%, claim self-reported — flag là **marketing claim**) chứng minh đây là capability quan trọng nhất cho agentic coding correctness; PTY drop/delay control sequence sẽ làm vỡ Claude Code giữa task ([full-terminal-use](https://docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/capabilities/full-terminal-use/)).

**NICE-TO-HAVE (để sau):**
4. **Block-based UI** (height-indexed SumTree, GridStorage cho active + FlatStorage cho scrollback) — cần parse OSC 133 ở client renderer. Hữu ích cho navigation + AI context framing, nhưng **không bắt buộc** để chạy Claude Code.
5. **MCP auto-discovery từ `~/.claude.json` / `.mcp.json`** — Warp đọc cùng config files của Claude Code, user đã config sẵn được integration free ([MCP docs](https://docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/capabilities/mcp/)). Trivial UX, no protocol work.
6. **Rich-content blocks coexist với terminal blocks** trong cùng BlockList (zero-height hidden blocks để collapse) — pattern elegant, hợp với SDK structured event stream.

**KHÔNG NÊN COPY:**
7. **Input editor của Warp** (buffered keystroke, multi-cursor) — incompatible với external-backend model của libghostty (render VT stream as-is). TUI input của Claude Code đã đủ.
8. **GPU renderer / custom UI framework của Warp** — bạn đã có libghostty Metal rendering.
9. **Oz cloud agent orchestration + Warp Drive cloud sync** — proprietary, out of scope cho P2P tool.

---

### 3. HERDR là gì + landscape multi-agent orchestration

#### 3.1. HERDR — CONFIRMED, định danh chính xác
[github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr) (tác giả Oğulcan Çelik). "Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal" — **terminal multiplexer Rust với agent awareness**, single binary. **3.6k stars, v0.6.6 (31/05/2026)** — cả hai số đều CONFIRMED qua live fetch. **Dual-license AGPL-3.0-or-later + commercial** (`hey@herdr.dev`) — CONFIRMED qua LICENSE file/Cargo.toml/nix ([herdr LICENSE](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/blob/main/LICENSE)). Chạy macOS/Linux, **không Windows** (phụ thuộc Unix socket).

Nó KHÔNG phải Claude Code plugin/framework. Kiến trúc: **background server quản workspaces/tabs/panes mỗi cái backed bằng real vt100 PTY**; thin-client connect local hoặc qua SSH. State tracking 3 signal: **process detection + socket API reports + screen heuristics**. Native integrate Claude Code (hook-based), Codex, OpenCode, Hermes, Pi, Qoder. Socket API = **NDJSON over Unix domain socket, không auth** — agent có thể create/destroy pane, read output, send keystroke, report state (blocked/working/done/idle), wait other agents, spawn helpers. `HERDR_ENV=1` báo agent biết đang chạy trong herdr; `SKILL.md` dạy Claude Code self-orchestrate.

> **⚠️ Lưu ý license load-bearing:** AGPL copyleft áp lên *software* herdr, KHÔNG lên *protocol*. Client Swift tự viết mà chỉ **speak NDJSON protocol** thì KHÔNG distribute herdr code → không tự động dính AGPL (protocol không copyrightable). AGPL chỉ trigger nếu: (a) ship binary herdr trong product, (b) link library herdr, (c) dùng source Rust của herdr. Commercial license (giá chưa công bố) giải quyết cả ba.

> **Đính chính nhận dạng:** "Herd" ([joinherd.ai](https://joinherd.ai/)) và "AgentHerder" ([agentherder.com](https://agentherder.com/)) là **project KHÁC**, đừng nhầm với herdr. "herdctl" ([edspencer.net](https://edspencer.net/2026/1/29/herdctl-orchestration-claude-code)) là tool MIT khác, Node.js, Docker-based, build trên Agent SDK.

#### 3.2. Landscape (universal pattern: git worktree isolation/agent + task queue + human review gate)

- **Claude Code Agent Teams** (native, experimental, `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`, v2.1.32+): lead session spawn N teammate (mỗi cái full session riêng context), shared task list file-locked + mailbox. **⚠️ Split-pane mode CONFIRMED KHÔNG support Ghostty** (chỉ tmux hoặc iTerm2) ([agent-teams docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams), [#24189](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24189) còn open). Blocker nằm ở phía Ghostty (thiếu stable CLI/IPC); [#26572](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/26572) đề xuất `CustomPaneBackend` protocol (JSON-RPC 2.0/NDJSON) sẽ unblock — chưa có phản hồi Anthropic.
- **Claude Squad** (smtg-ai, 7.7k★, Go, AGPL): tmux + worktree/agent, TUI dashboard.
- **Conductor** (conductor.build, macOS GUI, free BYOK): worktree/agent, diff-first review, không cần tmux.
- **Uzi** (devflowinc, Go, MIT): CLI `uzi prompt --agents claude:2,codex:1`, có `broadcast` + `checkpoint` cho sweep workloads.
- **Claude Code Agent Farm** (Dicklesworthstone, Python): 20-50 agent qua tmux panes, file-lock coordination (shared tree, không worktree).
- **Vibe Kanban** (Apache-2.0, community-maintained sau khi Bloop đóng cửa đầu 2026): Kanban-card-per-worktree.

#### 3.3. Có nên cho tool host/manage một herd of agents? — **CÓ, nhưng chỉ làm transport+rendering layer, ĐỪNG build orchestration product.**

Tool của bạn ở vị trí độc nhất để **host herd of Claude Code agents natively**, vì PTY-over-NetBird đã cung cấp core primitive mà tất cả tool kia build lên. Cơ hội cụ thể, theo thứ tự giá trị:
1. **Git worktree per agent** — mọi tool nghiêm túc đều dùng. Host support create/list/switch worktree (trivial shell) + expose mỗi worktree là một PTY session trong UI.
2. **Agent supervisor pane** (sidebar state blocked/working/done) — pattern chung của herdr/Claude Squad/Conductor. State detection dùng 3 signal như herdr: process detection + Claude Code hooks (Stop/TeammateIdle/TaskCompleted) + screen heuristics.
3. **HERDR compatibility** — speak socket protocol NDJSON natively, để client macOS/iPad render workspace/tab/pane model của herdr bằng ghostty surface. Bạn thành **first-class client** thay vì SSH terminal thường.
4. **Lấp gap Ghostty của Agent Teams** — vì Anthropic chưa support Ghostty split-pane, bạn có thể parse tmux pane-creation commands và translate sang multi-PTY model của mình; hoặc implement `CustomPaneBackend` (#26572) nếu nó land.
5. **KHÔNG NÊN làm:** full git-worktree UI, Kanban board, multi-model routing layer. Đó là product layer của herdr/Conductor/Claude Squad. **Giá trị của bạn = PTY transport + rendering quality (libghostty) + mobile client.** Hãy là *PTY host tốt nhất* cho các orchestration tool này, không replicate chúng.

---

### 4. KHUYẾN NGHỊ ROADMAP

#### Bây giờ (P0 — để Claude Code chạy mượt qua network PTY)
Đây là điều kiện đủ để Claude Code TUI hoạt động đúng — làm trước mọi thứ khác:
1. Set env trong PTY: `COLORTERM=truecolor`, `TERM=xterm-ghostty`. Nếu emit TERM custom → **bắt buộc** `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1` (DEC 2026 fix, CONFIRMED).
2. Bật fullscreen mặc định: `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`.
3. PTY bridge **bidirectional fidelity**: forward control sequences (Ctrl+C/D, Esc, double-Esc), kitty keyboard reports, SGR mouse, bracketed paste, OSC 8/52/777 **nguyên vẹn**. ĐỪNG strip/rewrite.
4. SIGWINCH forward + ioctl update, **debounce ~50ms**.
5. Document limitation: **image-paste qua remote PTY vỡ** (OSC 5522 chưa implement trong Ghostty — REFUTED claim "PR in progress"); workaround macOS Ctrl+V.

#### Sau (P1 — structured value)
6. **SDK-driven agent pane** (SwiftUI): drive Claude Code qua `claude-agent-sdk` stream-json, render tool cards / streaming text / permission buttons / cost statusbar. JSON over stdout chịu network buffering tốt hơn ANSI. Dùng `--bare`. Cảnh báo billing 15/06/2026 nếu dùng subscription auth (API-key thì miễn).
7. **OSC 133 shell-integration** inject trên remote host (libghostty đã parse sẵn) → block model cho shell commands quanh Claude Code: prompt-jump, exit-code coloring, per-block copy. Lưu ý Claude Code **chưa emit OSC 133 nội bộ** nên block grouping áp ngoài session.
8. **Claude Code lifecycle hooks** (kiểu claude-code-warp, handle cả OSC 777 `/dev/tty` lẫn `terminalSequence` stdout path) → status pane.

#### Sau nữa (P2 — multi-agent / orchestration)
9. **Agent supervisor sidebar** + git-worktree-per-agent PTY sessions.
10. **HERDR socket-protocol client** (NDJSON, tự viết — tránh AGPL bằng cách không embed binary/link library).
11. Lấp **Ghostty split-pane gap** của Agent Teams (parse tmux commands hoặc impl CustomPaneBackend nếu #26572 land).

#### Fit vào kiến trúc hybrid terminal+GUI
- **Terminal path (PTY/NetBird/libghostty)**: P0 + P1.7/P1.8 — là backbone. Block model + OSC 133 sống ở client-side VT parser của libghostty's external-backend.
- **GUI path (ScreenCaptureKit + VideoToolbox)**: orthogonal, dùng cho GUI windows; không đụng PTY layer.
- **SDK pane (P1.6)** là *tier thứ ba* nằm cạnh hai path trên: không phải PTY, không phải video — là native SwiftUI consume JSON stream. Đây chính là chỗ "rich-content blocks" của Warp map vào, và là nơi multi-agent supervisor (P2) hiển thị state.

#### Open questions còn treo (cần xác minh trước khi build các phần phụ thuộc)
- libghostty external-backend có handle đúng alt-screen switch (smcup/rmcup) khi remote gửi qua NetBird TCP không? (critical cho fullscreen mode).
- NetBird TCP có gây jitter/reordering làm vỡ ANSI sequence (OSC split across segments) không? Nếu có, cần framing/reassembly layer trước khi libghostty parse.
- libghostty external-backend expose **parsed escape-sequence stream** hay chỉ **rendered pixel buffer** cho host app (đặc biệt iOS/iPadOS)? Block detection cần cái trước.
- libghostty forward kitty keyboard reports as-is hay re-encode cho host OS? Quyết định Shift+Enter có work end-to-end không.

corpus.summary: Claude Code is a hybrid TUI/scrollback-based terminal app (Node.js/React-based renderer) that defaults to an inline scrollback mode but has an opt-in alternate-screen fullscreen mode. It is heavily dependent on terminal capabilities — truecolor, kitty keyboard protocol (for Shift+Enter and extended modifiers), bracketed paste, OSC 52 clipboard (for copy and image paste), OSC 8 hyperlinks, and mouse tracking in fullscreen mode. Over a high-latency network PTY it has documented severe latency sensitivity in its default streaming render path. An Agent SDK (Python + TypeScript library) and a headless CLI mode (claude -p) provide structured, programmatic integration well beyond just running the TUI — these are strong candidates for a dedicated agent pane in your remote-coding tool. The "herdr" project is real: it is an agent multiplexer TUI (Rust, AGPL) with native Claude Code state detection; "herdctl" is a separate MIT-licensed Docker-based orchestrator built on the Agent SDK.

points.claim: Claude Code renders in two modes: inline-scrollback (default) and alternate-screen fullscreen (opt-in)
points.detail: By default Claude Code appends output to the terminal scrollback buffer — it is NOT a full-screen TUI like vim or htop. On SIGWINCH (terminal resize) this design causes a well-documented bug: each resize event writes a new frame into scrollback without erasing the previous one, flooding scrollback with duplicate frames. The opt-in fullscreen renderer (CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 or /tui fullscreen, requires v2.1.89+) uses the alternate screen buffer exactly as vim does, keeping memory flat and eliminating the resize-flood bug. For a remote PTY client, fullscreen mode is strongly preferable: it isolates Claude Code's redraws from the host scrollback and enables mouse support. CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1 forces the classic renderer even when the setting is saved.

points.claim: Truecolor (24-bit RGB) is required; capability detection uses COLORTERM env var and a terminal-name allowlist
points.detail: Claude Code's UI elements (spinner, permission borders, diff backgrounds, statusline, subagent color chips, rainbow gradient for ultrathink/ultraplan) are hardcoded 24-bit ANSI escape codes — it does not fall back to 16-color ANSI automatically for all elements. The CLI detects truecolor capability from the COLORTERM=truecolor environment variable. Remote PTY connections frequently break this: the outer terminal may support truecolor but the remote shell does not advertise COLORTERM, causing flat/washed-out colors. Your remote client must either (a) propagate COLORTERM=truecolor into the PTY environment or (b) ensure the remote shell profile sets it. Claude Code v2.1.78 had a regression where 24-bit RGB on the statusline rendered washed-out. An accessibility option (dark-ansi / light-ansi base themes) exists for users who prefer 16-color ANSI.

points.claim: Kitty keyboard protocol (and DEC 2026 synchronized output) are probed from TERM; xterm-256color suppresses both
points.detail: Claude Code uses the TERM value to decide which extended keyboard features to enable. When TERM=xterm-kitty or xterm-ghostty it enables DEC 2026 synchronized-output sequences (which eliminate flicker during redraws) and the kitty keyboard protocol (which disambiguates Shift+Enter, Ctrl+Enter, and other modified keys). When TERM=xterm-256color, DEC 2026 is NOT sent, as confirmed by two open bug reports (issues #49584, #55613). A separate bug shows that xterm-ghostty terminfo's bracketed-paste declaration causes Claude Code to take a kitty-protocol paste path that can mangle multi-line pastes (issue #54700). For libghostty with its xterm-ghostty TERM value: expect kitty-protocol key handling and DEC 2026, but watch for the paste tokenization edge case. Emitting xterm-256color instead would suppress DEC 2026 but avoids the paste bug.

points.claim: Shift+Enter (multiline) requires the kitty keyboard protocol or a terminal-setup escape; Option/Alt+Enter also works after Meta-key enablement
points.detail: Enter always submits the prompt. To insert a newline without submitting: Ctrl+J works in every terminal unconditionally. Shift+Enter works natively in Ghostty, Kitty, iTerm2, WezTerm, Warp, Apple Terminal, and Windows Terminal. For VS Code, Alacritty, Cursor, and Zed, the /terminal-setup command writes the required escape sequences to the terminal config. gnome-terminal and JetBrains IDEs cannot support Shift+Enter at all. Option+Enter (macOS) requires Option-as-Meta enabled in terminal preferences. The documented Shift+Enter behavior requires that the PTY path correctly forwards the kitty extended key report or the VT220 CSI 27 modifyOtherKeys sequence — a raw PTY that strips these to bare Enter will break multiline entry.

points.claim: Key handling: Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, Esc, and double-Esc have specific Claude Code behaviors distinct from POSIX defaults
points.detail: Ctrl+C: if Claude is running a task, interrupts it; if nothing is running, first press clears the prompt input, second press exits Claude Code. Ctrl+D: exits the session (EOF signal). Esc: stops the current response or tool call mid-turn, keeping work done so far. Double-Esc: if prompt has text, clears it and saves draft to history; if prompt is empty, opens the rewind/checkpoint menu. In fullscreen mode: if a text selection is active, Ctrl+C copies instead of canceling. These are non-standard behaviors — your PTY client must NOT intercept or translate these sequences before Claude Code sees them.

points.claim: Mouse support is only available in fullscreen rendering mode; it uses standard terminal mouse tracking protocols
points.detail: In default scrollback mode, mouse is not used by Claude Code. In fullscreen mode, Claude Code requests mouse tracking from the terminal (SGR mouse protocol) and handles: click-to-position cursor in input, click-to-expand collapsed tool results, click-and-drag text selection (auto-copied to clipboard on release), scroll-wheel navigation, URL and file-path clicking. The CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=1 env var turns off mouse capture while keeping fullscreen rendering. Over SSH, clipboard writes fall back to OSC 52 escape sequences. Mouse wheel scrolling requires the terminal to forward mouse events — confirmed to work in Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm, iTerm2 (with mouse reporting enabled in profile). Your libghostty-based client must pass through mouse tracking reports from the remote PTY to the client surface.

points.claim: Image paste uses OSC 52 for clipboard read (text) and the Kitty OSC 5522 protocol for image MIME data; SSH breaks the xclip fallback
points.detail: Claude Code binds Ctrl+V (or Cmd+V in iTerm2, Alt+V on Windows/WSL) to paste-image-from-clipboard, which inserts an [Image #N] chip in the prompt. On macOS native, this shells out via platform APIs. On Linux without a display server (SSH/headless), it tries to spawn xclip — which fails because there is no display, creating a PTY race condition. An open issue (#42712) proposes implementing this entirely via OSC sequences: OSC 52 for text clipboard (already works over SSH in terminals that support it), and OSC 5522 (Kitty clipboard protocol with MIME type) for images. Terminal support for OSC 5522: Kitty (full support), Ghostty (PR in progress), iTerm2 and WezTerm (no image support). For your remote tool: libghostty already supports OSC 52 for text; if Ghostty adds OSC 5522 (image MIME), that path will work transparently through your PTY tunnel. Until then, image paste over SSH is broken except on Kitty.

points.claim: OSC 8 (hyperlinks) and OSC 52 (clipboard copy) are actively emitted by Claude Code; propagation through the PTY tunnel is required
points.detail: Claude Code emits OSC 8 hyperlink sequences to make file paths in tool output clickable. There was a v2.1.3 regression affecting OSC 8 links in custom statuslines (fixed in v2.1.42), and an open issue where OSC 8 works in IDE terminals but not in some real terminal emulators (Konsole). Claude Code uses OSC 52 for clipboard write operations — in the fullscreen renderer it writes selections to OSC 52 over SSH, but this requires the terminal to accept clipboard writes (iTerm2 blocks it by default until /terminal-setup enables it). Your PTY tunnel must pass OSC 52 and OSC 8 sequences unmodified from the remote end through to libghostty. tmux swallows these by default; set allow-passthrough on in tmux.conf if the intermediate hops involve tmux.

points.claim: Bracketed paste is used; large paste (>10,000 chars) collapses to a [Pasted text] placeholder; stdin cap is 10MB in -p mode
points.detail: Claude Code handles bracketed paste for normal text input. When pasted content exceeds 10,000 characters, the UI collapses the input to a [Pasted text] placeholder to keep the input box usable — the full content is still sent on submit. The VS Code integrated terminal can drop characters from very large pastes before they reach Claude Code. In headless/pipe mode (claude -p), piped stdin is capped at 10MB as of v2.1.128; exceeding it causes a clean exit with non-zero status. Your PTY client must forward bracketed-paste sequences (ESC [?2004h enable, ESC [200~ / ESC [201~ wrappers) from the remote PTY to the client terminal correctly.

points.claim: Terminal resize (SIGWINCH) is actively buggy in the scrollback renderer; fullscreen mode mitigates but does not fully fix resize artifacts
points.detail: In the default scrollback renderer, every SIGWINCH fires a full redraw that does NOT erase the previous frame, flooding scrollback with duplicate content (confirmed active in v2.1.110+ across macOS Terminal.app and JetBrains IDEs; issues #49086, #6481, #46462). v2.1.15 introduced a resize slowdown of 6-7 seconds in embedded terminals (issue #20094). In fullscreen mode the alternate screen buffer is used, which should isolate redraws, but CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1 does not fix the scrollback issue — meaning the bug occurs even when the alternate screen is active in some configurations. For your remote PTY: resize events must be forwarded (SIGWINCH sent to the process and the PTY ioctl updated); if the NetBird TCP path throttles or batches resize events, expect visual corruption. Recommend coalescing resize events with a short debounce (~50ms) before forwarding to reduce the number of SIGWINCH-triggered redraws.

points.claim: High-latency PTY connections cause severe UI lag in the serialized message path; this is a known documented issue
points.detail: Issue #20286 documents Claude Code (in VS Code Remote-SSH mode) becoming unusable at ~500ms RTT: permission dialogs appeared 30+ minutes late, streamed tokens piled up in a serialized Promise queue that waited for each message's round-trip before sending the next. The fix was to drop the await on postMessage and send updates immediately. For your native PTY approach (raw bytes over NetBird TCP), this specific VS Code extension serialization is not present — you are sending a raw byte stream, not serialized postMessage calls. However, the underlying issue remains: Claude Code's React-based renderer re-renders on every token, generating many small writes to the PTY fd. Over a 50-200ms WireGuard RTT, the terminal's flow control (XON/XOFF or TCP backpressure) may cause visible typing latency in the input box. Fullscreen mode (CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1) reduces the number of bytes sent per frame by only rendering visible messages, which directly helps over high-latency links.

points.claim: Agent SDK (Python + TypeScript library) provides full programmatic control — the deepest integration tier beyond just running the TUI
points.detail: The Claude Agent SDK (npm: @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk, pip: claude-agent-sdk) exposes a query() async generator that yields typed message objects: system/init (session metadata, plugins, tools), tool events, result messages. Features: structured JSON output with --json-schema validation, streaming via stream-json format, tool approval callbacks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks as in-process functions), subagent definitions, MCP server attachment, session resumption via session ID, --bare mode for reproducible CI runs that skip ~/.claude context loading. The TypeScript SDK bundles a native Claude Code binary as an optional dep — no separate Claude Code install required. This SDK tier enables your tool to drive Claude Code as a library: render its output in a structured pane, intercept tool use for UI display, and programmatically manage sessions without a PTY at all.

points.claim: Headless mode (claude -p) is the middle tier: CLI-driven, structured output, session continuity, no interactive TUI
points.detail: claude -p 'prompt' runs non-interactively: outputs text to stdout and exits. --output-format json wraps the result in a JSON object with session_id, total_cost_usd, model costs. --output-format stream-json emits newline-delimited JSON events including text_delta, tool invocations, system/api_retry, and system/init. --json-schema enforces a JSON Schema on the response, returning structured_output in the JSON envelope. --continue / --resume SESSION_ID maintain conversational context across invocations. --allowedTools pre-approves tools to skip permission prompts. --bare skips ~/.claude, .mcp.json, skills, plugins — recommended for reproducible scripted calls. As of June 15, 2026, -p usage on subscription plans draws from a separate monthly Agent SDK credit.

points.claim: Agent Teams (experimental) and subagents are native Claude Code multi-agent features; herdr and herdctl are third-party orchestrators
points.detail: Agent Teams (CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1, v2.1.32+): one lead Claude Code session spawns N teammate sessions, each with its own context window. Communication via a shared task list (file-locked JSONL in ~/.claude/tasks/) and a mailbox (direct inter-agent messaging). Split-pane display requires tmux or iTerm2. Teammate sessions are full Claude Code instances; they load CLAUDE.md and MCP servers from the project directory. Known limitations: no session resumption for in-process teammates, task status can lag, no nested teams, one team per lead. Herdr (github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr): Rust TUI terminal multiplexer, AGPL + commercial dual license. Detects Claude Code process state (blocked/working/done/idle) via process name and PTY output analysis without requiring hooks. Provides a socket API for agents to create workspaces, split panes, and monitor state. Herdctl (edspencer.net/herdctl): MIT-licensed, Node.js, built on the Agent SDK. Runs Claude Code agents in Docker, supports YAML-defined fleets with cron scheduling, Discord/Slack integration, and fine-grained tool permissions.

points.claim: Notification delivery uses OS desktop notifications (via OSC sequences) in Ghostty/Kitty/iTerm2, or falls back to terminal bell; tmux requires allow-passthrough
points.detail: When Claude Code finishes a task or needs permission input, it fires a notification event. Ghostty and Kitty forward this to the OS notification center natively; iTerm2 requires enabling 'Send escape sequence-generated alerts'. Other terminals (Warp, VS Code) must use preferredNotifChannel: 'terminal_bell' in settings.json, or a Notification hook (e.g., afplay on macOS). Desktop notifications travel transparently over SSH in Ghostty and Kitty. Inside tmux: set allow-passthrough on in tmux.conf — without it, notification escape sequences are swallowed. Your remote PTY client must allow these notification sequences to pass through to libghostty on the client side; since libghostty is Ghostty's rendering surface, it should handle them natively.

corpus.project_relevance:
For the libghostty-over-NetBird remote coding tool, the findings translate to concrete requirements and decisions:

MUST support (TUI path):
1. Propagate COLORTERM=truecolor into the remote PTY environment — otherwise Claude Code renders washed-out colors.
2. Set TERM to xterm-ghostty (libghostty's native value) — this enables DEC 2026 synchronized output and kitty keyboard protocol. Watch the bracketed-paste tokenization bug in xterm-ghostty; if it manifests, expose a toggle to switch to xterm-256color.
3. Forward kitty keyboard protocol reports unmodified — Shift+Enter, Option+Enter, and other modifier combos depend on this.
4. Enable fullscreen mode by default (CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1) — this is the only correct rendering mode for a remote PTY at any non-trivial latency. It uses the alternate screen buffer, reduces bytes-per-frame, and adds mouse support.
5. Forward mouse tracking reports from the remote PTY to libghostty — Claude Code in fullscreen mode requests SGR mouse tracking; libghostty on the client surface must receive those reports and render mouse interaction correctly.
6. Pass OSC 52 and OSC 8 sequences unmodified through the TCP byte stream — clipboard copy and clickable hyperlinks both depend on these reaching libghostty. Do NOT strip or rewrite these sequences in your tunneling layer.
7. Forward SIGWINCH on resize and update PTY ioctl — debounce resize events (~50ms) to reduce the per-SIGWINCH redraw flood; this is especially important over WireGuard where each redraw generates network traffic.
8. For image paste: OSC 5522 support in Ghostty is in progress. Once merged, image paste (Ctrl+V → [Image #N] chip) will work transparently over your PTY tunnel via OSC 5522. Until then, image paste is broken over SSH/remote PTY for non-Kitty users — document this limitation.
9. Forward Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, and Esc unmodified — these have Claude Code-specific behaviors and must reach the remote process unchanged.

SHOULD support (quality):
10. Allow-passthrough for notification OSC sequences — libghostty handles Ghostty-native desktop notifications natively, so this should be automatic.
11. Document that tmux at either end requires allow-passthrough on + set -s extended-keys on + set -as terminal-features 'xterm*:extkeys'.

SDK INTEGRATION DECISION — strongly worth pursuing for a dedicated agent pane:
The Agent SDK (Python/TypeScript) and claude -p headless mode provide a fundamentally different integration tier. Instead of pixel-pushing the TUI over a network PTY, you can embed claude-agent-sdk and drive Claude Code programmatically: render tool invocations as structured UI cards, show streaming text_delta events in a custom pane, expose permission approval as native UI buttons, display session cost and model in a status bar, and persist session IDs for resumption. The SDK's stream-json output maps naturally to a native Swift SwiftUI pane alongside the terminal pane. Recommended architecture: keep the raw PTY path for users who want the full interactive TUI experience; add an SDK-driven agent pane mode for structured, latency-resilient interaction (SDK output is JSON over stdout — much more tolerant of network buffering than raw ANSI sequences). The --bare flag ensures reproducible behavior in the SDK pane regardless of the user's ~/.claude configuration.
claims_to_verify: OSC 5522 (Kitty image clipboard protocol) Ghostty PR status — the Ghostty discussion #10099 and Claude Code issue #42712 both describe it as 'PR in progress' as of research time; verify current merge status before shipping the image-paste feature.

DEC 2026 synchronized output behavior with xterm-ghostty TERM value — issues #49584 and #55613 both report Claude Code 2.1.126 does NOT send DEC 2026 even on terminals that advertise support; this may have been fixed in a later release — verify against the current Claude Code version.

Agent SDK credit billing change (June 15, 2026) — the headless docs state claude -p on subscription plans will draw from a new monthly Agent SDK credit starting June 15, 2026; verify whether this affects your use case or if API-key auth is still unlimited.

Herdr dual licensing (AGPL + commercial) — the GitHub README states AGPL-3.0-or-later for open source; if integrating herdr's socket API or embedding herdr in your product, confirm AGPL compatibility or obtain commercial license.

Claude Code fullscreen mode and SIGWINCH resize artifacts — the CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1 workaround reportedly does NOT fix the scrollback duplication bug (issue #49086 says this); verify whether the current release (post-v2.1.110) has addressed this in the fullscreen renderer.
open_questions: Does libghostty's external-backend patch correctly handle alternate-screen buffer switches (smcup/rmcup) when the remote side sends them over the NetBird TCP stream? This is critical for fullscreen mode correctness.

Does the NetBird TCP path introduce enough jitter or reordering to cause visible ANSI sequence corruption (e.g., partial OSC sequences split across TCP segments)? If so, you need a framing layer or a buffer that reassembles sequences before libghostty parses them.

Will you expose the Claude Agent SDK integration as a first-class native Swift pane, or only as a TUI passthrough? The SDK approach would give you structured UI but requires you to ship the Node.js Claude Code binary or the Python claude-agent-sdk inside your app bundle.

How does libghostty handle the kitty keyboard protocol on the client side when the remote PTY emits kitty extended key reports — does it forward them as-is to the application layer, or does it re-encode them for the host OS? This affects whether Shift+Enter works end-to-end.

What is the status of the xterm-ghostty multi-line paste bug (issue #54700)? If it is unresolved in the current Claude Code release, you may need to expose a TERM override toggle in your client's settings.

verifications.claim: OSC 5522 (Kitty image clipboard protocol) Ghostty PR status — the Ghostty discussion #10099 and Claude Code issue #42712 both describe it as 'PR in progress' as of research time; verify current merge status before shipping the image-paste feature.

verdict.claim: OSC 5522 (Kitty image clipboard protocol) — Ghostty discussion #10099 and Claude Code issue #42712 both describe it as 'PR in progress' as of research time; verify current merge status before shipping the image-paste feature.
verdict.evidence:
The claim conflates two different issues and mischaracterizes the current state. The precise facts are:

1. GHOSTTY PR #10560 ("osc: parse OSC 5522 - Kitty clipboard protocol") was MERGED on February 16, 2026 into the 1.3.0 milestone. It was approved by both pluiedev and mitchellh, with 54 checks passing. Ghostty 1.3.0 shipped March 9, 2026. The PR author explicitly stated it "only adds support for parsing the OSCs" with "No support added to DECRQM/DECRPM/DECSET/DECRST for mode 5522." The feature is parse-only — not behaviorally implemented.

2. Ghostty DISCUSSION #10099 ("Support OSC 52 clipboard read for image data") is closed/answered but has NO merged implementation PR. It references PR #10089 (a GTK-side clipboard-content-check) but that is a separate, prerequisite concern. The collaborator pluiedev accepted the feature formally as issue #10549 with the comment "we can figure out how to impl this later."

3. Ghostty DISCUSSION #8275 (OSC 5522 tracking discussion) shows the feature was accepted as #10549, not yet implemented.

4. Claude Code ISSUE #42712 ("Support OSC 52/5522 clipboard for image paste over SSH") — this issue was NOT accessible via the GitHub CLI (returned "ISSUE NOT FOUND"), suggesting it may be private, deleted, or the number is incorrect. Its existence cannot be confirmed from authoritative sources.

SUMMARY: The PR (#10560) is MERGED but delivers parse-only support, NOT full implementation. No "PR in progress" state exists — the parse PR is done, and the implementation PR does not yet exist. The claim's framing of "PR in progress" is doubly wrong: it understates the parse PR (which is merged) and overstates the implementation (which has no PR at all). Shipping an image-paste feature that relies on Ghostty executing OSC 5522 semantically will fail — Ghostty will silently accept (parse) the sequence but not act on it.

BROADER RESEARCH FINDINGS (all three dimensions):

--- DIMENSION 1: Hosting Claude Code in a Network PTY Terminal ---

Claude Code is a native binary (x64/ARM64) installed to ~/.local/bin/claude. It is NOT a Node.js TUI wrapper — npm is only a distribution vehicle; the installed binary does not invoke Node. It requires a real PTY on Unix (ptyprocess/POSIX); Windows support uses a ConPTY equivalent.

TERMINAL REQUIREMENTS extracted from official docs (code.claude.com):
- Shells: Bash, Zsh, PowerShell, CMD. On Unix the tool invokes bash commands; on Windows Git Bash or PowerShell.
- Color: Auto-detected from the terminal. NO_COLOR / FORCE_COLOR in the shell env (not settings.json) control the UI palette. Themes: dark, light, dark-daltonized, light-daltonized, dark-ansi, light-ansi, plus custom JSON themes in ~/.claude/themes/.
- Width: Auto-detected from the PTY. A known bug was fixed where wrapped lines broke at exact terminal-width boundaries — meaning Claude Code queries the PTY dimensions at runtime.
- Rendering modes:
  * Default ("main-screen"): appends to normal scrollback; works over any PTY.
  * Fullscreen alt-screen ("tui: fullscreen" / CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1): uses alternate screen buffer; requires the terminal to support DEC alt-screen. Adds virtual scrollback, mouse tracking (disable with CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=1), and synchronized output (BSU/ESU — DEC private mode 2026).
- Escape sequences Claude Code actively uses: bracketed paste (mode 2004), synchronized output (mode 2026), focus events (mode 1004). Does NOT yet emit OSC 133 semantic prompt zones (multiple open feature requests: issues #1465, #22528, #26235, #32635).
- Notification channels: Native desktop notifications via Ghostty/Kitty/iTerm2 OSC sequences (preferredNotifChannel: "ghostty"/"kitty"/"iterm2"). Terminal bell fallback for all others including Warp, VS Code integrated terminal.
- Mouse: Claude Code tracks mouse in fullscreen mode. CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=1 disables this, restoring terminal native select-to-copy.
- Cursor: CLAUDE_CODE_NATIVE_CURSOR=1 uses the terminal's cursor rather than Claude's drawn block; CLAUDE_CODE_ACCESSIBILITY=1 for screen magnifier compatibility.
- Synchronized output: CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1 to force-enable for emulators (e.g., Emacs eat) that implement BSU/ESU but don't reply to the capability probe. No effect under tmux.
- tmux: requires allow-passthrough on + extended-keys on + terminal-features extkeys for notifications and Shift+Enter to work through.
- Shift+Enter: natively supported in Ghostty, Kitty, iTerm2, WezTerm, Warp, Apple Terminal, Windows Terminal. Requires /terminal-setup for VS Code/Cursor/Windsurf/Alacritty/Zed.

NETWORK PTY IMPLICATIONS for libghostty external-backend:
- Claude Code must run in a real PTY (it reads terminal dimensions, emits terminal escape sequences, uses alt-screen). A raw TCP byte-stream works if the transport layer correctly mediates PTY signals (SIGWINCH for resize, TIOCSWINSZ). The PTY must faithfully forward escape sequences including DEC private modes.
- libghostty (external backend) handles rendering on the client side. The host-side PTY byte stream should be passed unmodified. Critical: if the network layer strips or misinterprets escape sequences (especially OSC notifs or DEC modes), Claude Code features break silently.
- CLAUDE_CODE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN must be properly forwarded — libghostty's external backend needs to handle alt-screen switching (smcup/rmcup) just like it does for vim/less.
- The fullscreen (CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER) mode is the better mode for remote use: it uses virtualized scrollback, keeps memory flat, and renders to alt-screen — the same model libghostty uses for full-surface rendering.

AGENT SDK — headless/structured alternative to TUI:
The Claude Agent SDK (npm: @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk; pip: claude-agent-sdk) provides programmatic access to the same agentic loop without the TUI. It yields a stream of typed messages (SystemMessage, ResultMessage, tool use events). The TypeScript SDK bundles the native Claude Code binary as an optional dependency — no separate install needed. Key difference from just running `claude -p`: the SDK gives Python/TypeScript callbacks at every tool use (PreToolUse, PostToolUse hooks), session resumption via session_id, subagent definitions, and MCP server configuration — all structured, no terminal parsing required.
VERDICT on "is Agent SDK worth it beyond TUI?": YES for structured panes / orchestration features. The Agent SDK lets the remote tool render structured blocks (like Warp's rich content blocks) from the typed event stream rather than screen-scraping escape sequences. For the hybrid architecture described, a mixed approach is optimal: TUI path for interactive coding sessions (PTY + libghostty), Agent SDK path for orchestration panels, agent status, structured diff views.

--- DIMENSION 2: Warp Terminal Architecture ---

BLOCK MODEL: Warp's viewport is a BlockList — an ordered SumTree of typed Block objects. Terminal blocks wrap a command+output pair (two grids: command-side and output grid). Rich content blocks are arbitrary UI views. Key insight: the BlockList only needs each block's height, so terminal and rich-content blocks are composable without special-casing.
Storage: active regions use GridStorage (mutable rows-of-cells); immutable scrollback uses FlatStorage (packed byte buffer + row index) for lower memory. Virtualized rendering at two levels: viewport (only visible blocks) and row (only viewport-intersecting rows within a block).

SHELL INTEGRATION: Warp ships bootstrap scripts (bash, zsh, fish, PowerShell) that hook precmd/preexec and emit JSON-wrapped events inside a custom OSC envelope: `warp_send_json_message "{\"hook\": \"Preexec\", \"value\": {\"command\": \"...\"}}"`. The ANSI parser decodes these into a DProtoHook enum. Key events: Preexec (before command), Precmd (after, with pwd + git state + exit code). Protocol is extensible by adding enum variants and handler methods. Note: this is Warp's own proprietary protocol, not standard OSC 133.

AGENT MODE / ADE: Agent conversations appear as rich content blocks inline with terminal blocks. Agent shell commands still generate normal terminal blocks indistinguishable from user commands. View-layer filtering: hidden blocks carry zero height and are skipped by the renderer. Auto-detection routes natural language to agent mode; "!" prefix forces shell execution within agent contexts. Agent conversation blocks are scoped: they don't appear in the terminal block list unless explicitly attached.

TRANSFERABLE/INSPIRATIONAL FOR THIS PROJECT:
1. The block abstraction (height-indexed SumTree) is directly applicable to libghostty's surface model for structured panes.
2. The JSON-over-OSC shell integration protocol is a clean pattern for future hook points (prompt detection, exit codes, git state) without requiring OSC 133 which Claude Code doesn't emit yet.
3. Rich content blocks for agent reasoning/diffs alongside PTY blocks mirrors exactly what the Agent SDK structured event stream could power.
4. The "full BlockList in model, filtered view on screen" pattern — zero-height hidden blocks — is elegant for collapsing agent tool calls.

--- DIMENSION 3: herdr identification and multi-agent landscape ---

HERDR IS CONFIRMED: github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr (author: Oğulcan Çelik). It is an "agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal" — a terminal multiplexer with agent awareness. Written in Rust, single binary, no external dependencies. Latest release v0.6.6 (May 31, 2026), 43 total releases, 682 commits. Dual-licensed: AGPL-3.0-or-later + commercial.

KEY FEATURES:
- Terminal multiplexer: workspaces, tabs, panes, tiling — runs inside any existing terminal.
- Agent awareness: sidebar shows per-agent state (blocked/working/done/idle); workspaces roll up to most-urgent state for scanning.
- Built-in integrations with Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Pi, Hermes Agent, Qoder CLI (forward semantic state over socket API).
- Auto-detects 15+ agents including GitHub Copilot CLI, Grok CLI, Cursor Agent — via foreground process inspection and terminal output, zero config.
- SSH persistence: run on remote host, detach, reattach.
- Mouse-native: click/drag/split panes.
- 18 built-in themes (Catppuccin, Tokyo Night, Gruvbox variants).

RELEVANCE TO THIS PROJECT: herdr is the closest existing tool to what is being built — a terminal multiplexer with Claude Code integration. The socket API for semantic state (blocked/working/done) is particularly relevant. The key differentiation of this project (libghostty native rendering over NetBird WireGuard + ScreenCaptureKit GUI windows) goes far beyond what herdr does; herdr is a TUI-in-TUI approach whereas this project is a native terminal client.

BROADER MULTI-AGENT ORCHESTRATION LANDSCAPE:
- claude_code_agent_farm (Dicklesworthstone): orchestration for 20+ parallel Claude Code agents with tmux monitoring, lock-based coordination.
- ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator: parallel coding agents with CI fix automation.
- ruvnet/ruflo: multi-agent swarm platform with native Claude Code integration.

verdict.correction:
The claim has two errors:

ERROR 1 — "PR in progress": The Ghostty OSC 5522 parse PR (#10560) is NOT in progress — it MERGED on February 16, 2026 and shipped in Ghostty 1.3.0 (March 9, 2026). However, it only adds parsing, not behavioral implementation. The correct characterization is: "parse support merged in 1.3.0; implementation has no PR yet."

ERROR 2 — Claude Code issue #42712: This issue number could not be verified through any available source (GitHub CLI returned ISSUE NOT FOUND; it is possible the issue is private, the number is wrong, or it has been deleted). Do not rely on this issue number. The confirmed open requests for OSC 5522/clipboard-over-SSH are Ghostty issue #10549 and Ghostty discussion #10099.

SHIP DECISION: Do NOT ship an image-paste feature relying on Ghostty OSC 5522 semantics. As of Ghostty 1.3.0 (the latest release at research time), the terminal parses but silently ignores OSC 5522 sequences. There is no implementation PR open. The workarounds for image clipboard are: (a) xclip/wl-clipboard on Linux, (b) Ctrl+V (not Cmd+V) on macOS to bypass the terminal layer, or (c) wait for a future Ghostty implementation PR against issue #10549.

verifications.claim: DEC 2026 synchronized output behavior with xterm-ghostty TERM value — issues #49584 and #55613 both report Claude Code 2.1.126 does NOT send DEC 2026 even on terminals that advertise support; this may have been fixed in a later release — verify against the current Claude Code version.

verdict.claim: DEC 2026 synchronized output behavior with xterm-ghostty TERM value — issues #49584 and #55613 both report Claude Code 2.1.126 does NOT send DEC 2026 even on terminals that advertise support; this may have been fixed in a later release — verify against the current Claude Code version.
verdict.evidence: Both issues are confirmed real and accurately described. Issue #49584 was filed April 16 2026 against version 2.1.110, and issue #55613 against 2.1.126 — both confirmed by direct page fetches. The regression: starting at 2.1.110, Claude Code switched from dynamic capability detection (terminfo Sync cap or DECRQM queries) to a hardcoded TERM allowlist. DEC 2026 synchronized-output escape sequences are sent ONLY when TERM is exactly xterm-ghostty or xterm-kitty; terminals like eat-truecolor, xterm-256color (iTerm2), and any custom terminfo entry are silently skipped even when they advertise the Sync capability. Both issues are closed. The fix shipped in Claude Code v2.1.129 (May 6 2026) as a new env var: CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1 forces synchronized output on terminals that auto-detection misses. The current latest version is v2.1.159 (May 31 2026). The claim that the bug exists in 2.1.126 is confirmed; the partial fix (workaround env var, not full DECRQM/terminfo detection) shipped in 2.1.129. The root-cause issue (hardcoded list vs. dynamic detection) has not been reported as fully resolved — the env var is a workaround, not a dynamic detection fix. The claim itself says "may have been fixed in a later release" — this is partially true: a workaround exists but dynamic capability detection has not been restored.
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate as stated. Clarification: (1) The regression began at 2.1.110, not 2.1.126 — 2.1.126 is simply the version in the second duplicate report. (2) A workaround fix landed in 2.1.129: CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1 env var forces synchronized output for any terminal. (3) The underlying architectural issue (hardcoded TERM allowlist instead of dynamic DECRQM/terminfo detection) is not publicly reported as fully fixed as of v2.1.159 (current). (4) xterm-ghostty and xterm-kitty continue to receive DEC 2026 sequences natively without the env var. For a custom terminal (e.g., a native-Swift terminal with a novel TERM value), operators must set CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1 to receive DEC 2026 sequences from Claude Code.

corpus.summary: Warp is a Rust + GPU/Metal terminal that diverges fundamentally from VT passthrough emulators: it owns the input editor, groups output into semantically-typed blocks via injected shell hooks (precmd/preexec + DCS/OSC escape sequences carrying JSON metadata), and in 2025-2026 evolved into a full Agentic Development Environment (ADE) with native PTY-level agent execution, multi-agent management, MCP integration, and SSH Warpification. Key insight for our remote-coding tool: the block model's power comes entirely from shell integration signals, not from the renderer — meaning OSC/DCS-based prompt detection over a network PTY is both feasible and is the critical enabling primitive for everything Warp does on top of a terminal.

points.claim: Warp is NOT a PTY passthrough emulator — it owns the input editor and buffers keystrokes at the application layer
points.detail: Standard terminals relay keystrokes immediately over the PTY. Warp instead holds input in a full in-process text editor (SumTree/Rope data structure, multi-cursor, operation-based CRDT) and only writes to the PTY on Enter or Ctrl-C. This enables IDE-like editing (multi-cursor, syntax highlight, completions) before submission. The tradeoff: programs that expect raw keystroke streaming (e.g., vim inside Warp) still work because Warp passes through once a command is submitted, but the 'input editor' layer is not visible to the remote host at all — it lives entirely in the client. For our remote-coding tool this is directly relevant: libghostty renders the raw VT stream, so we do not intercept keystrokes this way — but it means Warp's input editor UX is a deliberate architectural choice, not a VT feature.

points.claim: Warp's GPU/Metal renderer uses custom Rust primitives (rect, image, glyph via texture atlas) rendered at 144+ fps — not a standard VT grid
points.detail: Built in Rust with a UI framework co-developed with Nathan Sobo (Atom co-founder), loosely inspired by Flutter. Renders via Metal on macOS, Vulkan on Linux, DirectX on Windows, with a future WebGL/WASM path. The renderer works at the level of rectangles, images, and glyphs rather than a character-cell grid. The critical architectural point: Warp forked Alacritty's Rust VT model as a starting point but diverged because a single VT grid cannot support isolated per-command blocks — two commands can end up on the same line in VT100, which blocks cannot allow. Warp uses a separate-grid-per-block model with GridStorage (mutable active region) and FlatStorage (packed immutable scrollback). A SumTree aggregates per-block heights for O(log n) viewport lookups. This rendering architecture is what Ghostty/libghostty also uses (Metal on macOS) — our tool already shares this foundation.

points.claim: Blocks are created via injected shell hooks (precmd/preexec) that emit DCS/OSC escape sequences carrying JSON metadata — NOT by the VT renderer inferring prompt boundaries
points.detail: Warp bootstraps bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell with scripts that hook into precmd (fires after command completes, carries pwd + git state) and preexec (fires before execution, carries the command string). These hooks call warp_send_json_message() which wraps a JSON payload in an escape sequence and writes it over the PTY. On macOS/Linux the sequence format uses DCS (Device Control String); on Windows, ConPTY swallows DCS so Warp switched to OSC (Operating System Commands) instead. The Rust ANSI parser decodes sequences into a typed DProtoHook enum (PreexecValue, CommandFinishedValue, etc.) which drives block demarcation. This means: blocks require cooperation from the shell running on the host. Over a plain network PTY without these hooks injected, Warp gets no block signals. The hooks are the integration layer — not the renderer.

points.claim: OSC 133 (A/B/C/D prompt markers) is the emerging open standard for the same shell-integration signaling that Warp does with its custom DCS/OSC protocol — and Claude Code itself has an open issue requesting OSC 133 emission
points.detail: OSC 133 defines four markers: A=prompt start, B=command start (end of prompt), C=command executed (output begins), D=command finished (with optional exit code). This is what iTerm2, Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm, and Windows Terminal all implement for semantic shell integration (prompt jumping, block selection, exit-code coloring). Warp uses a superset of this — its DCS/OSC protocol carries richer JSON payloads (git state, pwd, command metadata) beyond what OSC 133 provides. Ghostty (libghostty) already implements OSC 133 for its own shell integration across bash, zsh, fish, elvish, nushell. An open GitHub issue (anthropics/claude-code#22528) specifically requests Claude Code emit OSC 133 sequences. For our remote-coding tool: OSC 133 over the network PTY is the correct primitive to detect prompt boundaries and group output into blocks — it will work because these sequences flow through the byte stream unchanged over TCP/WireGuard.

points.claim: Warp's SSH Warpification: the client installs a remote-server binary (~/.warp/remote-server) and uses a length-delimited protocol over the same SSH connection — not a separate channel
points.detail: The legacy approach (now deprecated) installed tmux on the remote host and used tmux Control Mode as the side channel. The modern approach ships a matching remote-server binary for the remote OS+arch, communicates via a length-delimited multiplexed protocol over the single SSH connection, and exposes: shell hooks (blocks, completions), file tree sidebar, native file reads, inline code diffs. Features that work over Warpified SSH: blocks, input editor, completions, history, Agent Mode conversations, agent-executed shell commands, MCP integrations. Features not available: native code editor UI, computer use/screenshots, Windows remote hosts, non-bash/zsh shells. Relevance: our tool runs the PTY over NetBird (WireGuard TCP), which is a direct P2P connection — we do not need a separate side channel. Shell integration hooks injected on the remote will flow through our PTY stream exactly as they would locally. We do not need to replicate Warp's remote-server binary.

points.claim: Warp 2.0 ADE (June 2025): the block model enables coexistence of terminal blocks and rich-content blocks (agent reasoning, diffs, tool calls) in a single scrollable BlockList
points.detail: The BlockList is agnostic to block contents — it only needs each block's height for SumTree-based O(log n) viewport math. Terminal blocks (command + output) and rich-content blocks (agent conversations, code diffs, plan views) stack and scroll together. Agent-executed commands produce terminal blocks indistinguishable from user commands. Agent reasoning/UI produces rich-content blocks. The view layer can filter independently: in the conversation view, terminal blocks from agent runs appear as compact summaries (expandable on demand); in the terminal view, agent conversation blocks are hidden. This architectural separation is what makes the ADE feel unified rather than bolted-on. Key agent features: planning mode with o3-class reasoning models, multi-agent management panel (status of all running agents), Warp Drive (shared knowledge store for MCP configs, workflows, env vars), MCP auto-discovery from ~/.claude.json and .mcp.json.

points.claim: Full Terminal Use: Warp agents interact at the raw PTY level — reading the live terminal buffer, writing bytes (including control sequences like Ctrl+C), and sustaining long-running interactive processes (REPLs, debuggers, git rebase)
points.detail: This is the capability that won Warp #1 on Terminal-Bench (52% task completion, 20+ percentage points ahead of next). The agent can: read the live PTY buffer in real time, write arbitrary bytes including control sequences, maintain interactive processes without timeout. This prevented the common failure mode of git pager trapping agents. Configuration: per-action approval, session-level auto-approve, or always-allow. The agent uses Claude Sonnet 4 as base model with Claude Opus 4 for planning. For our remote-coding tool: Claude Code already runs in a PTY and handles interactive processes — what matters is that our PTY bridge faithfully streams bytes bidirectionally including control sequences, which NetBird TCP does. No special PTY-level agent API is needed beyond what the Claude Code TUI does natively.

points.claim: Warp has an official Claude Code integration plugin (warpdotdev/claude-code-warp) that uses OSC 777 escape sequences written to /dev/tty to deliver notifications and session state from Claude Code hooks into Warp's UI
points.detail: The plugin hooks six Claude Code lifecycle events: SessionStart, Stop, Notification, PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse. Each hook runs a shell script that builds a JSON payload and writes OSC 777 sequences (format: \033]777;notify;<title>;<body>\007) to /dev/tty — the controlling terminal device — rather than stdout, so delivery works even with output redirection. Warp parses these to drive inline status indicators and its notification center. The on-stop.sh hook extracts the first user prompt and last assistant response from the JSONL transcript for the completion notification. This is the current integration surface between Claude Code and Warp — and it is lightweight, not requiring any structural changes to Claude Code itself. Our remote-coding tool can implement the same OSC 777 / /dev/tty delivery mechanism since our PTY bridge passes the byte stream through intact.

points.claim: Warp auto-discovers MCP configs from ~/.claude.json and .mcp.json (Claude Code's config files) — making Warp and Claude Code share the same MCP server setup with zero extra configuration
points.detail: Warp reads MCP server definitions from: ~/.warp/.mcp.json (global), .warp/.mcp.json (project), ~/.claude.json (global Claude Code), .mcp.json (project Claude Code), ~/.codex/config.toml (Codex), .codex/config.toml, ~/.agents/.mcp.json. Global Warp servers auto-spawn; third-party agent servers require explicit enablement; project-scoped servers require per-session approval. The /agent-add-mcp built-in skill lets agents extend their own MCP tooling from within a conversation. For our tool: this auto-discovery means users already configured for Claude Code get Warp MCP integration for free — a UX pattern worth copying.

points.claim: Warp is now open source (April 2026): client under AGPL v3, UI framework (warpui_core, warpui crates) under MIT. Server-side infrastructure, Oz cloud agent orchestration, and AI credit system remain proprietary.
points.detail: The GitHub repo is warpdotdev/warp. The dual-license model (MIT UI framework + AGPL client) means the rendering/block code is inspectable but any redistribution of the full client must be AGPL. Oz (cloud agent orchestration) and the credit-based AI features are closed. The revenue model is credits for AI features + Pro tier ($12/month) + enterprise. OpenAI is listed as founding sponsor. Relevance: the AGPL license means we cannot embed Warp's client code directly without AGPL compliance — but the protocol and architectural patterns (OSC-based shell integration, block model, DCS JSON payloads) are implementable independently.

points.claim: What is transferable for our remote-coding tool (must-have vs nice-to-have)
points.detail: MUST-HAVE: (1) OSC 133 shell integration over the network PTY — inject hooks on the remote host; signals flow through NetBird TCP unchanged; enables prompt detection, command grouping, exit-code coloring with zero renderer changes. This is the enabling primitive. (2) Claude Code lifecycle hooks (claude-code-warp pattern) — write OSC 777 to /dev/tty for notifications; SessionStart/Stop/PermissionRequest map directly to our UI state machine. (3) Bidirectional PTY fidelity including control sequences (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+Z) — Warp's Terminal-Bench win proves this is non-negotiable for agentic coding. NICE-TO-HAVE: (4) Block-based UI grouping command+output — requires parsing OSC 133 signals in the client renderer; useful for navigation (jump between commands) and AI context framing but not required to run Claude Code. (5) MCP auto-discovery from ~/.claude.json — trivial UX improvement, no protocol work. NOT WORTH COPYING: (6) Warp's input editor (buffered keystrokes, multi-cursor) — incompatible with libghostty's external-backend model; ghostty renders the VT stream as-is. Claude Code's own TUI input is sufficient. (7) Warp's GPU renderer / custom UI framework — we already have libghostty with Metal rendering. (8) Warp's proprietary Oz cloud agents — out of scope for our P2P remote tool. (9) Warp Drive cloud sync — a product feature, not a protocol.

corpus.project_relevance: Our remote-coding tool (Swift/libghostty + NetBird PTY bridge) needs exactly one protocol primitive to unlock Warp-style block intelligence: OSC 133 shell integration injected on the remote host. Because our PTY runs over NetBird TCP (direct P2P), OSC/DCS escape sequences flow through the byte stream unchanged — no side channel, no remote-server binary required, unlike Warp's SSH approach. Concretely: (1) Inject OSC 133 A/B/C/D hooks into the remote shell (bash/zsh/fish scripts, same as Ghostty's existing shell integration); parse them in our Swift client to build a block model. This enables command grouping, exit-code indicators, prompt-jump navigation, and per-block copy/share — all the Warp block UX. (2) Ship the claude-code-warp hook set (or an equivalent) on the remote host: six shell scripts that emit OSC 777 JSON payloads to /dev/tty on Claude Code lifecycle events (SessionStart, Stop, PermissionRequest, etc.). Parse these in the client to drive a Claude Code status pane — 'agent running', 'needs permission', 'task complete' — without any structural changes to Claude Code itself. (3) Ensure the PTY bridge passes control sequences bidirectionally (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+Z, Ctrl+D, arbitrary byte writes) — Warp's Terminal-Bench result proves this is the single most important capability for agentic coding correctness; a PTY that drops or delays control sequences breaks Claude Code mid-task. Do NOT attempt to replicate: Warp's buffered input editor (libghostty renders the VT stream as-is, which is correct for Claude Code's own TUI), Warp's proprietary Oz cloud orchestration, or Warp's rich-content block renderer (the terminal block model via OSC 133 is sufficient and simpler).

verifications.claim: Warp uses DCS (not OSC 133) as its primary shell integration protocol on macOS/Linux — the exact escape sequence format (DCS vs custom OSC code) should be verified against the open-sourced warpdotdev/warp repo now that it is AGPL-licensed

verdict.claim: Warp uses DCS (not OSC 133) as its primary shell integration protocol on macOS/Linux — the exact escape sequence format (DCS vs custom OSC code) should be verified against the open-sourced warpdotdev/warp repo now that it is AGPL-licensed
verdict.evidence:
Multiple converging sources confirm this:

1. DCS IS the primary protocol on macOS/Linux. Warp's own blog post "How Warp Works" states: "Using these hooks, we send a custom DCS (Device Control String) from the running session to Warp. This DCS contains an encoded JSON string that includes metadata about the session." The Warpify subshells documentation shows the exact DCS sequence format: `printf '\eP$f{"hook": "SourcedRcFileForWarp", "value": { "shell": "zsh"}}\x9c'` — i.e., `\eP` (DCS start) + `$f{JSON}` + `\x9c` (ST). GitHub issue #8755 confirms the bootstrap sequence is a DCS-encoded JSON blob: "Warp collects all shell aliases at bootstrap time and encodes them into a DCS terminal escape sequence to send metadata to the UI (warp_bootstrapped)."

2. On Windows, Warp SWITCHED to OSC. "Bringing Warp to Windows" blog post states: "ConPTY was swallowing the DCS messages from the shell…[so] we updated the shell and the terminal to communicate via OSCs instead of DCSs." This means DCS = primary macOS/Linux; OSC = Windows fallback/replacement.

3. OSC 133 is NOT Warp's internal protocol. Warp issue #6718 shows OSC 133 A sequences from users' shells BREAK Warp's output rendering — confirming Warp does not consume or depend on OSC 133 markers; they conflict with its own DCS-based block detection.

4. AGPL repo verification: The warpdotdev/warp repo IS public under AGPL v3 (as of April 28, 2026). However, the shallow clone reveals only build scaffolding (Cargo.toml, WARP.md, app/DockTilePlugin, CI configs) — the full Rust terminal source (`app/src/terminal/`, parser code) is not yet in the public tree as of the clone date. The WARP.md confirms the architecture references `app/terminal/` as the location for "Terminal emulation and shell management," but this directory is absent from the publicly checked-out tree. **The Rust source that implements DCS parsing cannot be directly code-verified from the public repo at this time.** The protocol claim is confirmed from official documentation and blog posts, not from source inspection.

SUMMARY: DCS on macOS/Linux (confirmed), custom OSC codes on Windows (confirmed), OSC 133 not used by Warp internally (confirmed by breakage when users inject OSC 133 markers).
verdict.correction: The claim is confirmed with one important nuance: on Windows, Warp migrated FROM DCS TO custom OSC codes (after forking ConPTY) because ConPTY swallows unrecognized DCS sequences. So the statement "DCS is the primary protocol" is correct for macOS/Linux but not for Windows. The claim about verifying against the AGPL source is partially achievable — the repo is public but the terminal emulation Rust source files are not yet in the public tree, so the protocol can only be verified from official documentation and blog posts, not from source code inspection.

verifications.claim: The claude-code-warp plugin uses OSC 777 written to /dev/tty — confirmed by deepwiki synthesis of the repo; spot-check the actual hook scripts at github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp to verify the exact sequence format before implementing

verdict.claim: The claude-code-warp plugin uses OSC 777 written to /dev/tty — confirmed by deepwiki synthesis of the repo; spot-check the actual hook scripts at github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp to verify the exact sequence format before implementing
verdict.evidence: The actual source of plugins/warp/scripts/warp-notify.sh (verified via Morph github_codebase_search against the live repo) confirms the exact sequence format: `\033]777;notify;<title>;<body>\007` where `\033]` is the OSC introducer, `777` is Warp's custom command number, `notify` is the subcommand, and `\007` is the BEL terminator. However, the claim that /dev/tty is always the delivery path is now PARTIALLY OUTDATED. As of Claude Code v2.1.141, the plugin uses a version-negotiated dual path implemented in emit-terminal-sequence.sh: (1) on CC >= 2.1.141 it emits `{terminalSequence: <seq>}` JSON to stdout instead of writing to /dev/tty, because older CC's Stop hook validator rejects unknown JSON fields; (2) on CC < 2.1.141 it writes to /dev/tty; (3) unknown version tries /dev/tty first and falls back to JSON. The deepwiki synthesis described only the /dev/tty path, which was the original design but is no longer the sole path. The OSC format itself (`\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON-body>\007`) is confirmed correct.
verdict.correction: The OSC 777 format and /dev/tty write are confirmed for Claude Code < 2.1.141. On CC >= 2.1.141 (current), the sequence is instead delivered via a `terminalSequence` JSON field on stdout, bypassing /dev/tty entirely. Any implementation must handle both paths. The exact format is: `\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON-body>\007` where the body is a JSON object with fields: v (protocol version), agent, event, session_id, cwd, project, and event-specific fields.
