verdict.claim: Warp's remote-server SSH extension uses a 'length-delimited protocol over a single SSH connection' — the exact wire format is undocumented; verify against the open-sourced repo or GitHub discussions before designing our own multiplexing
verdict.evidence: The Warp docs at docs.warp.dev/terminal/warpify/ssh/ state verbatim: "The SSH extension supersedes this by speaking a length-delimited protocol over a single SSH connection." The documentation says generators run in parallel over this single multiplexed connection (solving MaxSessions ceiling problems), and that a remote-server binary is installed under ~/.warp/remote-server on the remote host. However, the exact wire format spec (framing, message types, serialization format) is NOT documented anywhere in the public docs, GitHub discussions, or the open-sourced warpdotdev/warp repo. Warp open-sourced its client UI crates (MIT/AGPL v3) but the SSH remote server protocol implementation details remain proprietary — there is no public spec, no protocol buffer schema, and no community documentation of the message format. The GitHub discussion thread #442 on warpdotdev/warp covers remote connection management at a user level only. The claim is therefore confirmed: the phrase 'length-delimited protocol over a single SSH connection' appears in Warp's own docs, and the exact wire format is indeed undocumented in any publicly accessible source.
verdict.correction: No correction needed — the claim is accurate. The phrase appears verbatim in Warp's official SSH docs. The wire format is undocumented in the open-sourced code; designing your own multiplexing protocol independently is the correct approach rather than trying to reverse-engineer or reuse Warp's.

corpus.summary: Herdr (github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr) is CONFIRMED and correctly identified: it is a Rust-based terminal-native agent multiplexer — "one terminal for the whole herd." It is NOT a Claude Code plugin or orchestration framework per se; it is a PTY-first session manager with agent-state awareness and a Unix socket API, sitting between tmux and a purpose-built agent IDE. The broader multi-agent Claude Code orchestration landscape in 2025-2026 is rich and rapidly maturing: at least a dozen active tools exist using git-worktree isolation as the near-universal primitive, with TUI dashboards (Claude Squad, Uzi), native GUI Mac apps (Conductor, Herd), hook-based async patterns, and Claude Code's own experimental Agent Teams feature. For a remote-coding tool, the most actionable patterns are: PTY-per-agent with worktree isolation, a supervisory sidebar/dashboard showing agent state (blocked/working/done), and hook-based merge/review gates — all of which map well to a NetBird-connected multi-PTY host architecture.

points.claim: Herdr is confirmed: a Rust terminal multiplexer for agent supervision, 3.6k stars, actively maintained (v0.6.6 released May 31 2026).
points.detail: Repo: [ogulcancelik/herdr](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr). Author: Ogulcan Celik. Written in Rust (90.7%). License: AGPL-3.0 + commercial option. The tagline is 'one terminal for the whole herd.' It is NOT a pure Claude Code orchestration framework — it is a PTY session server with agent-awareness bolted on. Architecture: background server process manages workspaces/tabs/panes each backed by a real PTY; a thin-client connects (locally or over SSH); state is tracked via three signals: process detection, socket API reports from agent integrations, and screen heuristics. Natively integrates with: Claude Code (hook-based), Codex (hook-based), OpenCode (JS plugin), Hermes Agent, Pi, OMP, and Qoder CLI. Release cadence is very high (43 releases; 5+ releases in May 2026 alone), covering: git worktree support in CLI+socket API (v0.6.2), native agent session restoration (v0.6.3), Claude Code permission-prompt fixes (v0.6.6), and CJK IME support. Runs on macOS and Linux; does NOT run on Windows (Unix socket dependency). Distributed as single Rust binary via curl script, Homebrew, or Nix flake.

points.claim: Herdr's key differentiator: real PTY processes (not rewritten view), Unix socket API for agent self-orchestration, and SSH thin-client attachment.
points.detail: Each pane embeds a real vt100 PTY — the same terminal the agent sees natively. This is architecturally identical to what a remote-coding tool would want: the host runs real PTYs, the client renders them. The socket API allows agents (including Claude Code via a SKILL.md file) to: create/destroy panes, read pane output, send keystrokes, report their own state (blocked/working/done/idle), wait for other agents, and spawn helpers. The HERDR_ENV=1 env var signals to agents they are running inside Herdr. Remote attach: SSH into the host, run `herdr --remote`, and any SSH client works — no native app required. Image paste over SSH is supported (staged as temp files). The agent skill file ([SKILL.md](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/blob/master/SKILL.md)) is a markdown doc that teaches Claude Code (and other agents) how to self-orchestrate: split panes, read output, spawn helpers, wait on dependencies — activating only when HERDR_ENV=1 is set.

points.claim: Herdr vs. tmux vs. GUI agent IDEs: it occupies a deliberate middle position — no full GUI, no browser, but more agent-aware than tmux.
points.detail: Compared to tmux: Herdr adds semantic agent state (blocked/working/done/idle shown in sidebar), a structured socket API agents can write to, built-in session persistence with restore (scrollback survives restart), and agent-session resume via native CLI identifiers. Compared to GUI agent IDEs (Conductor, Herd): herdr stays terminal-native, works over SSH without any app, preserves user's shell/fonts/keybinds, and avoids the Mac-only limitation. One noted limitation: no built-in browser, which some visual-inspection workflows need. It is AGPL with commercial licenses available — not pure MIT open-source.

points.claim: Adjacent identity clarification — 'Herd' (joinherd.ai) and 'AgentHerder' (agentherder.com) are DISTINCT projects from herdr.
points.detail: [Herd](https://joinherd.ai/) is a cross-platform desktop app (v0.2.1, Windows/macOS/Linux) for visual multi-agent orchestration — a lightweight agent IDE with a real-time dashboard for dozens of parallel agents including Claude Code and Codex. No GitHub link publicly listed. It is NOT the same as herdr. [AgentHerder](https://agentherder.com/) is a training/certification curriculum for 'the professional practice of orchestrating fleets of AI coding agents,' with an associated open-source tool called cctabs. Also unrelated to herdr.

points.claim: Claude Code's native Agent Teams feature (experimental, v2.1.32+) is the official Anthropic-first orchestration primitive.
points.detail: Enabled via CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1. Architecture: one 'team lead' Claude Code session spawns teammates (each is a full independent Claude Code session with its own context window). Shared task list with file-locking for atomic claim. Teammates communicate via mailbox messaging — not only through the lead. Display modes: in-process (Shift+Down to cycle) or split-panes (tmux or iTerm2 required — notably NOT Ghostty). Team config stored in ~/.claude/teams/{name}/config.json. Hooks: TeammateIdle, TaskCreated, TaskCompleted for quality gates. Known limitations: no session resumption for in-process teammates, one team at a time, no nested teams, split-pane mode does not work in Ghostty. Subagents (different from Agent Teams) are lighter — fan out focused tasks, report only to caller, no inter-agent messaging. Best use cases for teams: parallel code review, competing debugging hypotheses, cross-layer feature work. Recommended team size: 3-5 teammates, 5-6 tasks per teammate.

points.claim: Claude Squad (smtg-ai/claude-squad) is the most-starred terminal orchestrator: 7.7k stars, Go, tmux + git worktrees, AGPL-3.0.
points.detail: Each agent gets: an isolated git worktree (own branch, no cross-contamination) and a dedicated tmux session. TUI dashboard with vim-style navigation (j/k), Enter to attach/re-prompt, 's' to commit+push, 'c' to checkout. Supports Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Aider, OpenCode, Amp via configurable launch profiles. Auto-accept 'yolo' mode for unattended runs. Latest release v1.0.18 (May 23 2026). Installable via Homebrew as `cs`. Requires tmux and GitHub CLI as prerequisites.

points.claim: Conductor (conductor.build) is the leading macOS GUI orchestrator: free, BYOK, git worktree per agent, diff-first review UI. Made by Melty Labs (YC S24).
points.detail: Mac-only (Apple Silicon), free download. Each workspace is a new git worktree. Supports Claude Code and Codex (plans to expand). Dashboard shows all agents' progress and blockers in real-time. Built-in diff viewer for reviewing changes before merge/PR creation. Uses existing Claude authentication (API key or Pro/Max plan subscription) — no separate billing. Version 0.61.1 as of 2026. Does NOT require tmux.

points.claim: Uzi (devflowinc/uzi) is the CLI-first parallel agent runner: 579 stars, Go, MIT, worktree+tmux+port isolation, with broadcast and checkpoint commands.
points.detail: CLI tool: `uzi prompt --agents claude:2,codex:1 "task"` spawns N agents each in own git worktree + tmux session + dynamic port allocation (for dev servers). Key commands: `uzi ls` (monitor), `uzi auto` (auto-confirm tool calls), `uzi broadcast` (send instruction to all agents simultaneously), `uzi checkpoint` (commit + rebase all agent branches back to main). v0.0.2 released June 3 2025. The broadcast+checkpoint pattern is well-suited for 'sweep' workloads: same task sent to many agents, best result cherry-picked or merged.

points.claim: Claude Code Agent Farm (Dicklesworthstone) runs 20-50 agents in parallel via tmux panes with file-lock coordination and real-time dashboards: 840 stars, Python/Shell.
points.detail: Two-script architecture: Python orchestrator manages tmux sessions, spawns agents, monitors health, restarts failures; Shell viewer displays live dashboard. Lock-based coordination uses .coordination/agent_locks/ directory with 2-hour stale-lock timeout and atomic file operations. Agents independently pick from a pool of 'problem files.' Supports 34 tech stacks, 37 prompt templates, 35 best-practice guides. Real-time dashboard shows agent state, context usage %, heartbeat time, cycle count. Requires Python 3.13+, tmux, Claude Code. The 'agent farm' pattern differs from worktree isolation: all agents share the same working tree but use file locks to prevent collisions.

points.claim: Vibe Kanban (BloopAI/vibe-kanban) introduced the Kanban-card-per-worktree pattern; now community-maintained after Bloop's early 2026 shutdown.
points.detail: Apache-2.0 licensed. Each Kanban card maps to a git worktree + agent session. Web-based UI with MCP-driven task decomposition and built-in browser preview. Solved the 'doomscrolling gap' — 2-5 minutes when an agent is working with no user action needed. Bloop (the company) shut down early 2026; the open-source project continues community-maintained.

points.claim: The universal orchestration pattern is: git worktree isolation per agent + task queue + human review gate before merge. Six architectural variants exist.
points.detail: Tier 1 (in-process): Claude Code Agent Teams, subagents — 1-3 agents, interactive, shared terminal. Tier 2 (local TUI): Claude Squad, herdr, Uzi — 3-20 agents, tmux sessions, dashboard sidebar, human-in-loop. Tier 3 (local GUI): Conductor, Herd — 3-20 agents, visual dashboard, worktree per agent, diff-first review. Tier 4 (agent farm): Claude Code Agent Farm — 20-50 agents, file-lock coordination, sweep workloads. Tier 5 (cloud async): Claude Code Web, GitHub Copilot agents — assign and review PR later. Tier 6 (specialized orchestrators): ccswarm (role-specialized agents), Bernstein (planning-to-merge pipeline), Composio agent-orchestrator (CI-aware). The Ralph Loop (pick task → implement → validate → commit → reset context) is emerging as a best-practice stateless agent execution pattern to prevent context accumulation bugs.

corpus.project_relevance: Our remote-coding tool is uniquely positioned to HOST a herd of Claude Code agents natively, because our PTY-over-NetBird architecture already provides the core primitive all these tools are built on top of. Concrete opportunities: (1) HERDR COMPATIBILITY: herdr's remote-attach model (SSH in, `herdr --remote`) is essentially what we are building — except we could be a first-class client rather than an SSH terminal. Support the herdr socket protocol natively and let the macOS/iPad client render herdr's workspace/tab/pane model with our ghostty surface. The HERDR_ENV integration points (blocked/working/done state forwarding over socket) map directly to a supervisory sidebar we would want to build anyway. (2) GIT WORKTREE MANAGEMENT: every serious orchestration tool uses one git worktree per agent. Our host must support creating, listing, and switching worktrees (trivial shell commands) and exposing each worktree as its own PTY session in the client UI. (3) AGENT SUPERVISOR PANE: the 'sidebar showing agent state' pattern (herdr, Claude Squad TUI, Conductor dashboard) translates to a dedicated panel in our macOS/iPad UI showing all running Claude Code PTYs, their state, and quick-action buttons (interrupt, prompt, approve). State detection can use the same three signals herdr uses: process detection, Claude Code hooks (Stop/TeammateIdle/TaskCompleted), and screen heuristics. (4) CLAUDE CODE AGENT TEAMS: the split-pane mode explicitly requires tmux or iTerm2 and does NOT support Ghostty currently — this is a direct gap we can fill by implementing the pane-split signaling protocol Claude Code expects. If Anthropic adds Ghostty support, our tool would benefit automatically; alternatively, we can parse the tmux pane creation commands and translate them to our own multi-PTY model. (5) HOOK-BASED GATES: implement the Claude Code hook event system (Stop, TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted) on the host side, forwarding hook events over our NetBird channel to the client, which can show approval UI for plan-approval or merge gates without the user needing to interact with the raw terminal. (6) WHAT TO SKIP: building a full git worktree UI, a Kanban board, or a multi-model routing layer — these are product layers better left to herdr/Conductor/Claude Squad. Our value is the PTY transport + rendering quality (libghostty) + mobile client. We should focus on being the best remote PTY host for these orchestration tools, not replicating them.

verifications.claim: Herdr's AGPL-3.0 license and commercial license availability — confirm exact terms before integrating the socket protocol in a proprietary product

verdict.claim: Herdr's AGPL-3.0 license and commercial license availability — confirm exact terms before integrating the socket protocol in a proprietary product
verdict.evidence: The herdr LICENSE file at github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr (commit 043968d) states exactly: "Herdr is dual-licensed: 1. Open source: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 or later (AGPL-3.0-or-later). See the full text below. 2. Commercial: commercial licenses are available for organizations that cannot comply with AGPL. Contact hey@herdr.dev for details." The Cargo.toml also declares license = "AGPL-3.0-or-later". The nix package descriptor sets license = lib.licenses.agpl3Plus. All three sources are internally consistent. The socket protocol itself is NDJSON over a Unix domain socket with no built-in auth — it is entirely local filesystem-level access. A proprietary product that embeds herdr binaries, links against herdr libraries, or distributes herdr as part of its product would be subject to AGPL copyleft (which requires source disclosure for any networked service). A product that merely *speaks the socket protocol* from its own independently-written client code is not incorporating herdr code and thus not automatically subject to AGPL — the protocol itself is not copyrightable. The commercial license is available but its pricing and exact scope are not published; contact hey@herdr.dev is the only disclosed path.
sources: https://herdr.dev/docs/socket-api/ — protocol spec (NDJSON over Unix socket, no auth layer documented)
verdict.correction: The claim is confirmed as stated but needs one important nuance: AGPL-3.0 copyleft applies to the *software* (herdr itself), not to the socket *protocol* as a communication format. A proprietary remote-coding client that implements the protocol from scratch in its own Swift/Rust/other code is not distributing herdr code and is therefore not automatically subject to AGPL obligations. The risk surface is: (1) shipping the herdr binary inside the product, (2) statically or dynamically linking herdr libraries, or (3) using herdr's Rust source in derived works — all three trigger AGPL. The commercial license (price undisclosed, contact hey@herdr.dev) resolves all three. Herd (joinherd.ai, a separate GUI multi-agent IDE) is a different product with no disclosed license and should not be confused with herdr.

verifications.claim: Claude Code Agent Teams' explicit exclusion of Ghostty in split-pane mode — verify against latest Claude Code changelog; this may change given Ghostty's growing adoption

verdict.claim: Claude Code Agent Teams' explicit exclusion of Ghostty in split-pane mode — verify against latest Claude Code changelog; this may change given Ghostty's growing adoption
verdict.evidence: The live Claude Code agent-teams documentation (fetched directly from code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams, as of June 2026, covering releases up to v2.1.157) states verbatim in the Limitations section: "Split-pane mode isn't supported in VS Code's integrated terminal, Windows Terminal, or Ghostty." Split-pane mode requires either tmux or iTerm2 with the it2 CLI. The Week 22 (May 25–29 2026) changelog covering the most recent release batch (v2.1.150–v2.1.157) contains zero mention of Ghostty, teammate-mode changes, or new pane backends. GitHub issue #24189 ("Add Ghostty as a split-pane backend for agent teams") remains open as of Feb 8 2026 with no official Anthropic comment or assigned milestone. The blocker is architectural: Ghostty lacks a stable CLI/IPC mechanism; macOS AppleScript/App Intents and Linux D-Bus integrations are in development by the Ghostty team. GitHub issue #26572 proposes a CustomPaneBackend protocol (JSON-RPC 2.0 over NDJSON) that would decouple agent teams from tmux entirely and unblock Ghostty, WezTerm, Zellij, and remote deployments — filed Feb 18 2026, open, no Anthropic response visible. A community workaround using Ghostty 1.3+ AppleScript to create split panes exists (gist by @lukehedger) but the author explicitly notes it does NOT integrate with Claude Code's teammateMode. The claim is confirmed: Ghostty is still explicitly excluded from split-pane mode in the current canonical docs, and no changelog entry has changed this through the latest release.
sources: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/24189 (Ghostty feature request, open as of Feb 8 2026)
verdict.correction: The exclusion is confirmed current, not historical. However the claim's implication that it "may change" is directionally correct: issue #24189 is tracking it, and issue #26572 proposes a protocol that would enable Ghostty support without requiring Anthropic to hard-code a Ghostty backend. The blocker is on the Ghostty side (stable IPC API), not an Anthropic policy decision. Once Ghostty ships macOS AppleScript/App Intents or Linux D-Bus support, the path to implementation is clear.
